text: The surgeon begins a linear approach to excise an ischiorectal lipoma in the gluteal region of a 37-year-old woman. The CT scan reveals a 15-centimeter mass in the ischiorectal fossa, with no signs of infiltration or intratumoral vascularization.

The patient is positioned in a jackknife position for a perineal minimally invasive approach. The surgeon makes a semicircular incision in the anodermal skin and uses a long star retractor for better exposure. Electrocautery is used to dissect the subcutaneous tissue, localizing the external sphincter. The mass is carefully dissected with bipolar energy, preserving the sphincter apparatus. The tumor is separated from the obturator fascia and obturator internus using digital dissection, avoiding bleeding. Once fully separated, the mass is eviscerated.
The final size of the tumor was fourteen point eight plus seven point two centimeters. Definitive pathology exam was a giant lipoma without infiltration of resection margins.

The surgeon removes the long star retractor and sutures the ischiorectal fossa with single stitches using 2-0 Vicryl. The skin is approximated with a continuous intradermal suture using 3-0 monofilament.
